title: Horrific Crash Ends Jeddah Sprint on Final Lap as Keisner Wins from Pole
meta: Published: 25 April 2026 | By Car Fok, Conventional Race Chronicler & Ex‑Ferrari Driver | F1CC News
caption: Robin Keisner took a commanding sprint victory in Jeddah, but the race was overshadowed by a violent final‑lap crash.
body:
Robin Keisner won the Saudi Arabian Grand Prix sprint race from pole position, but the result was overshadowed by a violent last‑lap collision that brought the session to an early end under red flags. Richard Hicks, Jason Roy, and Quarter Kevu were all involved in the incident at the final sector, with Roy's Red Bull sustaining significant floor damage that will rule him out of qualifying for the main grand prix. All three drivers emerged from their cars without injury.

Keisner's victory was the second of his sprint career and his first since Shanghai. The Audi driver controlled the race from the start, building a lead that peaked at over eight seconds as the soft‑tyre runners around him began to fade. By the time the red flag was thrown on the final lap, Keisner was untouchable — a performance that reaffirmed his status as one of the grid's most consistent performers, even as the chaos unfolded behind him.

The sprint began under clear, hot conditions — 30 degrees ambient, 43 on track — with Keisner converting pole into an immediate lead. Quarter Kevu challenged briefly into Turn 1, but the Audi pulled clear. Behind them, a strategic split emerged: the majority of the field started on soft tyres, but a handful of drivers — including Jason Roy, Philippe Verhaeren, Riley Swansson, and Sarah Springfield — opted for mediums. The gamble was that the softs would fade and the mediums would come alive in the closing laps. For Roy, the strategy looked poised to pay off until the final lap.

The race's first major incident came on lap 6, when Philippe Verhaeren lost control on his own at Turn 22 and hit the wall. The Red Bull driver was unhurt, but the damage was severe — a wheel torn from the car. The incident triggered a Virtual Safety Car, which bunched the field and gave the soft runners a reprieve from degradation. At the restart, the race ignited. Roy locked up and lost momentum, allowing Kevu and Maikel Zwart to sweep past. Zwart, on softs, produced a stunning double overtake to climb to second place, his Mercedes looking racy in the high‑speed conditions. Behind him, the medium runners — led by Roy and Swansson — began their charge.

As the laps ticked down, the soft tyres began to fade and the medium runners closed in. Roy, Swansson, and Nathanial all made gains, with Swansson passing Zhao Lei for eighth. The top eight compressed into a DRS train, every position contested. With two laps remaining, Zwart was fighting to hold second place, Roy was charging on his mediums, and Hicks had climbed to fourth after a series of audacious moves — including a side‑by‑side battle with Roy that saw the Audi driver briefly take third before Zwart repassed him.

The final lap was chaos. Kevu attempted a divebomb on Roy into Turn 1 but couldn't make it stick. Behind them, the pack was three wide at points, drivers braking later and later. Into the final sector, Hicks made a move on Roy. The two touched, Kevu was collected, and all three cars were launched into the barriers. The red flag was thrown immediately. Keisner, cruising to victory nearly nine seconds up the road, crossed the line under the red flag conditions, with the final classification taken from the running order at the moment of the crash.

The stewards reviewed the incident and handed Richard Hicks a 5‑second time penalty for causing a collision. The penalty did not affect the sprint result, as the classification was taken at the red flag. However, the consequences for the main grand prix are severe. Jason Roy will miss qualifying due to the floor damage sustained in the crash. Red Bull mechanics face a race against time to rebuild the car for Sunday, but with significant structural damage, Roy may be forced to start from the pit lane.

The sprint result strengthens Keisner's grip on third in the championship, while Zwart's second place is a welcome result for a Mercedes team that has struggled for form. Kevu, classified after the red flag, collects points but will rue the incident that denied him a potential podium. The paddock now turns its attention to qualifying, where Roy's absence opens the door for the midfield to claim an unexpected grid slot. Jeddah, as always, has left its mark.
